summary: [.Net] Long freeze when clicking the `Android` export template with projects that have lots of gdignored files
Scaled `Node3D`  and  `Node2D` scene roots always show a warning

description.text:
### Tested versions

Reproducible in 4.4.stable

### System information

Godot v4.4.stable - Windows 11 (build 22631) - Multi-window, 1 monitor - Vulkan (Forward+) - dedicated NVIDIA GeForce GTX 1660 SUPER (NVIDIA; 32.0.15.6636) - Intel(R) Core(TM) i7-10700F CPU @ 2.90GHz (16 threads)

### Issue description

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/070c67cb-274f-46d3-816d-66ac29bb508b)

I know this behaviour was probably added to 4.4 and intended, but I'm reporting it as a bug because IMO it is inappropriate. Rotated/scaled scene roots are a very legitimate use case and users should not need to be unconditionally warned about something they do on a regular basis without any means of turning off the warning. Seeing a bright yellow icon in the scene tree is very distracting, and if the user learns to simply ignore it, they risk ignoring legitimate warnings on the same node.

Many assets have a default root rotation or scale, which can optionally be overridden in containing scenes, or by inherited scenes. Some examples:
- A book lying on its side
- A cloud which has a default scale but can be made smaller or bigger

I think this warning might make slightly more sense if it were just in response to the _position_ being non-zero, since that will *always* be overridden.

### Steps to reproduce

- Scale any `Node3D` or `Node2D` scene root.
- Observe the warning that appears.

### Minimal reproduction project (MRP)

N/A

summary: Confusing getter behavior in the debugger inspector

description.text:
### Tested versions

- Reproducible in v4.4.stable.mono.official [4c311cbee]

### System information

Godot v4.4.stable.mono - macOS Sonoma (14.6.1) - Multi-window, 3 monitors - Metal (Forward+) - integrated Apple M2 Max (Apple8) - Apple M2 Max (12 threads)

### Issue description

The Debugger's inspector doesn't always call the getters, creating confusing errors when inspecting invalid objects during a debugging session.

I've experienced this error in PhantomCamera; see [Issue 487](https://github.com/ramokz/phantom-camera/issues/487).

Current behavior: sometimes the debugger calls the class's getter, and sometimes it doesn't.

Expected: the debugger's inspector always calls the getters.

Maybe:
- Catch exceptions and flag the value in the inspector,
- Provide a property similar to `PROPERTY_USAGE_NONE` for the debugger's inspector.

### Steps to reproduce

If you run this code, and set a breakpoint, the debugger's inspector will display "e = 0" (does not call the getter).
 
```gdscript
extends Node2D

var repro: Object = null

var e: int:
	set = set_e,
	get = get_e

func set_e(value: int) -> void:
	repro.e = value
	
func get_e() -> int:
	print_debug("E GETTER!")
	return repro.e

func _ready():
	print_debug("create a breakpoint here!")
```

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/d0eff057-a1d0-471d-a844-9e0113dcd4cd)

If you wrap the same code in a class, the behavior seems different:

In that example, set a breakpoint, then try to inspect the `repro` object with the debugger's inspector, the getter is called and throws a nil exception:

```gdscript
extends Node2D

var repro: Repro

class Repro:			
	var repro: Object = null

	var e: int:
		set = set_e,
		get = get_e

	func set_e(value: int) -> void:
		repro.e = value
		
	func get_e() -> int:
		print_debug("E GETTER!")
		return repro.e

func _ready():
	print_debug("create a breakpoint here!")
	repro = Repro.new()
	print_debug("inspect the repro member to trigger the nil error!")
```

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/5ec36b4f-37d8-4aa1-b9b5-30fcf3ef1e70)

The debugging experience gets confusing, because this behavior feel a bit unpredictable, and exceptions start to pop out from nowhere (the debugger doesn't show up in the stacktrace).

### Minimal reproduction project (MRP)

See examples above

summary: C# Rendering Device might dispose resources at wrong thread when using Compositor Effect

description.text:
### Tested versions

- Reproducible in v4.4.stable.mono
- Not producible in v4.3.stable.mono

### System information

Godot v4.4.stable.mono - Windows 11 (build 22631) - Multi-window, 2 monitors - Vulkan (Forward+) - dedicated NVIDIA GeForce RTX 4070 (NVIDIA; 32.0.15.6094) - AMD Ryzen 9 7900X 12-Core Processor (24 threads)

### Issue description

I'm making a rendering effect just like Octopath Traveller's in-house scene, which rendering in-house scene and out-house scene simultaneously, such as 

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/67c6ead9-12fc-43e0-887a-a51a9ed28bb8)

I use two SubViewport to render the inside and outside scene, and use a Compositor Effect to capture the depth texture of the inside scene for final mixing. 

However, after I do a main scene transition (unloads all nodes including house and outside scene), and when the next time I do transition back to this scene, Godot Editor reports Bugs as follows (which repeats around 30+ times for one scene transition)

```
E 0:00:22:180   GodotObject.base.cs:137 @ void Godot.GodotObject.Dispose(bool): This function (free) can only be called from the render thread. 
  <C++ Error>   Condition "render_thread_id != Thread::get_caller_id()" is true.
  <C++ Source>  servers/rendering/rendering_device.cpp:5995 @ free()
  <Stack Trace> GodotObject.base.cs:137 @ void Godot.GodotObject.Dispose(bool)
                GodotObject.base.cs:100 @ void Godot.GodotObject.Finalize()
```

I'm not sure how to reproduce it in a MRP, because it seems this bug report triggers from C# `GodotObject.Dispose()`. I've tried use `GC.Collect(GC.MaxGeneration, GCCollectionMode.Forced);  GC.WaitForPendingFinalizers();` manually in MRP, but it cannot reproduce the bug report. 

I suspect this bug report is corresponding to the Compositor Effect. Because the bug report only appears after I have entered the house and created the Compositor Effect. 


### Steps to reproduce

1. make a scene (which calls SCENE_A), add Compositor Effect to a camera.
2. do scene transition to another scene. 
3. do scene transition again, and editor will report bugs. 

### Minimal reproduction project (MRP)

[test_free_rid.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/19698928/test_free_rid.zip)

In this MRP, press 'F' to turn on/off Depth Capture, press 'Esc' to switch scene and trigger GC. Debugger will report Errors if Depth Capture has been turned on once.

Closes #104225, closes #81199

summary: Implement alignment (size-relative offset) for sprite nodes

description.text:
<!--
Please target the `master` branch in priority.

Relevant fixes are cherry-picked for stable branches as needed by maintainers.

To speed up the contribution process and avoid CI errors, please set up pre-commit hooks locally:
https://docs.godotengine.org/en/latest/contributing/development/code_style_guidelines.html
-->

Closes https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/9222.

Introduces a new property to all sprite nodes (Sprite2D, AnimatedSprite2D, Sprite3D & AnimatedSprite3D) that acts an offset relative to (i.e multiplied by) the texture dimensions. This provides a convenient way to easily align pivots to any corner or border of the sprite without needing to take size into account, in a way that still works well alongside the existing `centered` property (for compatibility, even though that property is arguably made redundant by this feature).

The property can be set to any vector2, but to support the intended common case the slider in the inspector has the range needed to allow aligning to any corner and no more -- which means -0.5..0.5 when `centered` is true, and -1.0..0 when it's false. For example, this means you can easily align a sprite's pivot to its center-bottom by dragging the `alignment.y` slider to the leftmost position

I'm not entirely sure on the name `alignment`, but it's the best I could think of that is succinct and clearly distinguishes it from `offset`.

This MR implements an (incompatible) alternative solution to parts of https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/93538

summary: Spatial shader srgb -> linear hints not working for NoiseTexture2D

description.text:
### Tested versions

- Reproducible in Godot 4.4 stable
- Tested in different versions, seems to be even going back to Godot 3
- Did not find a version with a different behaviour

### System information

Godot v4.4.stable - Windows 10 (build 19045) - Single-window, 2 monitors - Vulkan (Forward+) - dedicated NVIDIA GeForce RTX 4080 (NVIDIA; 32.0.15.6636) - AMD Ryzen 7 3700X 8-Core Processor (16 threads)

### Issue description

`NoiseTexture2D` is not correctly rendered in linear colorspace when being used in a **spatial shader**.
This is broken for the default `StandardMaterial3D` etc, but also does not work when using a custom shader.


The texture is not converted from srgb -> linear, no matter what uniform hints are used.


The problem of this bug comes up when a `NoiseTexture2D` is converted to an image to improve performance by avoiding noise calculation at runtime. Also multiple `NoiseTexture2D` can be converted to a single image file to pack them into channels and avoid unneccessary texture lookups in a shader.


The following image illustrates the issue.
![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/66d0420b-7872-4e3a-97eb-c67214f3e7d8)


## Exclaimer

I am aware that maybe it is not the correct use of `source_color` when using NoiseTexture2D in a shader. But the above is definitely not the expected behaviour as every other Texture2D format is correctly displayed either in linear or srgb colors space when using the uniform hints or not.

I also chatted with @clayjohn about this issue and he confirmed that it is not expected behaviour.

## Challenge / Problem

Fixing this bug straight away would potentially break a lot of vfx materials on live projects. I have seen hundreds of shaders that use NoiseTexture2D + `source_color` and building effects with it. Changing this behaviour would change the appearance of those effects.


Maybe that's ok and an exclaimer in the versions release notes mentioning the neccessary removal of the hint would be good.

### Steps to reproduce

- Create a Material on a MeshInstance3D + Quad
- Add a NoiseTexture2D to the albedo slot.
- Convert to shader
- Change the albedo textures uniform hints, observe no change.

### Minimal reproduction project (MRP)

[NoiseTexture2Dhints.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/19273581/NoiseTexture2Dhints.zip)

summary: CanvasGroup has unexpected zoom behaviour when using fit_margin
Environment background exhibits colorful noise when using physical or procedural skies

description.text:
### Tested versions

v4.4.stable.official [4c311cbee]

### System information

Godot v4.4.stable - Windows 10 (build 19045) - Multi-window, 2 monitors - Vulkan (Forward+) - dedicated NVIDIA GeForce RTX 2060 (NVIDIA; 32.0.15.6094) - AMD Ryzen 5 5600X 6-Core Processor (12 threads)

### Issue description

Environment backgrounds exhibit a colorful noise pattern. This becomes more obvious when using auto exposure. At default exposure it's harder to spot, but definitely still visible and noticeable.

This pattern doesn't play well with auto exposure, seemingly being ignored by auto-exposure adjustments. It is more obvious when using auto exposure with a dark the skybox, through either not having e.g. a directional light, through a low ISO setting, or decreasing the sky intensity.

summary: Update BoneAttachment3D transform when setting the bone index

description.text:
This PR fixes an issue encountered by the Blender Project DogWalk team where BoneAttachment3D nodes were not reading the transform of their attached bones when the bone was set. This is rarely noticeable in practice because there are other events that can cause the transform to update, such as opening the scene in the editor, or playing an animation.

However, the transform was not being updated during import of 3D scenes such as glTF files. If you viewed the imported scene in the editor, it would appear fine, because the editor causes the transform to update. But when directly using an imported glTF scene with a non-animated skeleton, the bug occurs.

Also, even if you do edit and save the scene using editable children or inherited scenes, this will create dozens of entries in that new scene since the BoneAttachment3D transform is considered different than the base file, causing version control noise, and more importantly, headaches where the transform is unexpectedly overridden to an older value. Imagine how frustrating it would be for people if they change the transform in Blender and nothing happens in Godot because Godot decided to overwrite the transform with the same value thanks to this bug.

summary: Tooltips reappear after being closed

description.text:
### Tested versions

Reproduced in v4.5.dev3.official [55fc6e73f], v4.4.stable.official [4c311cbee], v4.3.stable.official [77dcf97d8] and v1.0.stable.custom_build
(it says custom_build but it's the win64 binary I just downloaded)


### System information

Godot v4.4.stable - Windows 11 (build 22631) - Multi-window, 1 monitor - OpenGL 3 (Compatibility) - Intel(R) Iris(R) Xe Graphics (Intel Corporation; 32.0.101.5768) - 11th Gen Intel(R) Core(TM) i7-1185G7 @ 3.00GHz (8 threads)

### Issue description

This issue is concerned with the behaviour of tooltips and the rules that govern *when a tooltip should appear*. I've edited this issue in an attempt to clarify this.

When a tooltip is dismissed/closed (hidden in response to user input), *no user input is required to cause it to appear again*.
Specifically, tooltips will reappear without the mouse being moved at all.
The behaviour occurs in running projects as well as the editor and affects all versions of Godot, as far as I can tell.

The expected behaviour, and that of typical desktop software requires the cursor to exit the bounds of the element that triggered the tooltip, before the tooltip *is allowed* to be shown again. I say *"is allowed"* because the other rules still apply -- the rules that governed the initial appearance of the tooltip, i.e. mouse lingers over the element.

In the video, a tooltip for a node in the scene tree appears and I press the Escape key to dismiss it. The mouse/cursor is not moved (other than at the start/end). The tooltip reappears and Escape is pressed again. This is repeated several times.

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/ffde8d68-5348-4782-ae75-9d1005d4f72c

### Steps to reproduce

1. hover mouse over node in scene tree
2. wait. a tooltip will appear
3. press escape to dismiss the tooltip
4. wait. a tooltip will appear

summary: 3D Physics Interpolation: AnimationPlayer causes instance_set_transform warning when animating the position of a physics-interpolated node

description.text:
### Tested versions

Reproducible in 4.4.stable

### System information

Godot v4.4.stable.mono - Windows 10 (build 19045) - Multi-window, 2 monitors - Vulkan (Forward+) - dedicated NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3060 (NVIDIA; 32.0.15.6636) - Intel(R) Core(TM) i5-9400F CPU @ 2.90GHz (6 threads)

### Issue description

If physics interpolation is enabled, animating a Node3D's position with an AnimationPlayer causes the following warning to appear roughly every 15 seconds:
`W 0:00:15:015   instance_set_transform: [Physics interpolation] Interpolated instance triggered from outside physics process: "/root/Node3D/Cube" (possibly benign).
  <C++ Source>  servers/rendering/renderer_scene_cull.cpp:1032 @ instance_set_transform()`

This is happening because AnimationPlayer's `callback_process_mode` defaults to "Idle", which is equivalent to moving the object during `_process()` instead of `_physics_process()`.  While that would normally be ill-advised, I would expect an exception to be made in the case of AnimationPlayer; after all, if the user is animating `position`, then they obviously want the animator to handle any interpolation.

There are workarounds to make this warning go away, but they all have unacceptable downsides.

### Workaround 1:  Set the callback mode to "Physics".
This is the "obvious" solution, but it has a lethal drawback: it makes skeleton animations look choppy, since armatures aren't affected by physics interpolation.  See `WhyCallbackModeIsBadFix.tscn` in my example project for a demonstration.

### Workaround 2: Disable physics interpolation for the object being animated.
This doesn't work if the object's _parent_ is using physics interpolation, since [3D physics interpolation is performed on the _global_ transform instead of the _local_ one](https://docs.godotengine.org/en/4.4/tutorials/physics/interpolation/2d_and_3d_physics_interpolation.html).  If you try this, then the object being animated will appear to jitter, since its parent is being interpolated but it itself isn't.  See `WhyDisablingInterpolationIsBadFix.tscn` for a demonstration.

### Workaround 3: Don't animate a node's position.  Ever.
Or its rotation.  Or its scale.  This is only an option for models with a skeleton.  If you're animating something _without_ a skeleton, you're out of luck.

### Workaround 4: Ignore the warning, embrace chaos
This is what I'm currently doing.  It's making me warning-blind, though.

### Steps to reproduce

1. Create a Node3D
2. Animate its `position` with a looping AnimationPlayer
3. Turn on physics interpolation
4. Wait 15 seconds.  The warning will appear.

### Minimal reproduction project (MRP)

[AnimatorPhysicsInterpBug.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/19266223/AnimatorPhysicsInterpBug.zip)

summary: Using an autoload in a custom texture used in a Theme that is set as the default theme causes a crash

description.text:
### Tested versions

- v4.4.stable.official [4c311cbee]
- v4.2.2.stable.official [15073afe3]

### System information

Godot v4.4.stable - Windows 11 (build 26100) - Multi-window, 1 monitor - Vulkan (Forward+) - integrated Intel(R) UHD Graphics - Intel(R) Core(TM) i5-1035G1 CPU @ 1.00GHz (8 threads)

### Issue description

When a `Theme` is set as the default theme (using the project setting `gui/theme/custom`), and a custom resource is set as the value of one of its icons, if that custom resource uses an autoload anywhere, the parser will say that the autoload does not exist. In a bit more detail:

Say we have an autoload named `Autoload`, and a custom resource defined as follows:
```gdscript
extends Texture2D
class_name CustomIcon

func _init() -> void:
    Autoload # we get a warning about this line not doing anything but this attempts to access the autoload
```

We now create a `Theme` and add an instance of the above resource as the value of an icon. We set the `Theme` as the default theme using `gui/theme/custom` in the project settings.

If we now try to run the game, we will be treated with a parser error saying `Parser Error: Identifier not found: Autoload`. In addition, we get errors saying `debug: Condition "_try_capture(command, data, captured) != OK" is true. Continuing.`.

This crash can also occur when the custom resource doesn't directly use an autoload, since my main project currently suffers from that (though it is on `4.2.2`), probably when the resource indirectly loads a script that does use an autoload.

I presume the parser error occurs because the resource's script is parsed before the autoloads are loaded. The other errors probably occur because the game crashes before Godot is able to properly load the debugger (I've had this error before where it was caused by a breakpoint in a specific location, removing the breakpoint removed the errors).

### Steps to reproduce

See above or the MRP

### Minimal reproduction project (MRP)

[MRP.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/19265044/MRP.zip)

summary: FPS is locked to 60 on Android 15, even with a 120 Hz display

description.text:
### Tested versions

godot4.3rc

### System information

Android 15 with Snapdragon 8gen2

### Issue description

Dear Developers,

Hello. I noticed that after exporting to Android 15 using Godot 4.3 (in compatibility mode, i.e., OpenGL), the frame rate is locked at 60 FPS during runtime. This occurs regardless of whether vertical sync is enabled. I tested this on Android 15 devices such as the Xiaomi 13 and VIVO X100, both of which have 120Hz refresh rate screens. I confirmed that high refresh rate display is enabled in the device settings (additionally, when switching from the background to the foreground, the frame rate briefly hits 120 FPS but rapidly drops to 60). You can see this video: https://www.tiktok.com/t/ZT2bHo3HU/

Just make a small scene with a label on the top left of the screen and make it show fps by gdscript like this:

func _process(delta):

summary: Camera exposure adjustments cause changes in ambient lighting contribution

description.text:
### Tested versions

v4.4.stable.official [4c311cbee]

### System information

Godot v4.4.stable - Windows 10 (build 19045) - Multi-window, 2 monitors - Vulkan (Forward+) - dedicated NVIDIA GeForce RTX 2060 (NVIDIA; 32.0.15.6094) - AMD Ryzen 5 5600X 6-Core Processor (12 threads)

### Issue description

Adjusting camera exposure adjusts ambient light contribution. This causes significant unexpected changes in value relations of the scene, e.g. completely crunching shadows, or egregious brightening of ambient lighting. This makes camera exposure practically unreliable.

This is the case for both the practical and physical camera attributes, and also regardless of whether `physical light units` is used or not.

You may need to ensure your screen brightness is set high enough for these examples.

| | Overexposed | Underexposed |
| - | - | - |
| Blender Cycles | ![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/c471206a-800a-49fb-a9ce-92901c3065ae) | ![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/08e67554-909c-426e-a987-e25f066fece0) |
| Camera exposure | ![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/95b31f7d-a526-4e1c-a719-2e6f2ba481c4) | ![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/6cb4716f-33bf-4761-94e1-799ad8823ad4) |
| Tonemap exposure | ![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/36ce5c75-1835-4606-a6d6-d9869bb658c3) | ![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/72cefca3-36a5-448d-ac2a-ac4eb8a5c985) |

| Blender Scene baseline | Godot Scene baseline |
| - | - |
| ![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/08796653-ee46-4595-aa26-019897bc0ca4) | ![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/a3b29d17-f56d-4a4a-b41a-841cce1423be) |

Notable comments:
- Camera underexposure entirely crunches shadows into pure blackness.
- Camera overexposure completely removes any notion of shadow.
- Changing camera exposure completely loses relations between values compared to baseline.
- Tonemap exposure gives expected results, doesn't adjust ambient lighting contribution, and keeps the value relations between light/dark as expected. Generally, the tonemap exposure adjustments are much closer to what we see happen in the Cycles comparison, as well as what you'd get from similar adjustments on a real-life camera.

The exposure comparison in Blender is eyeballed, but vastly in favor of Godot (I.e. the bright parts of the underexposed image are darker than the brights in Godot).

Ambient contribution from reflection probes do not exhibit this same behavior, and respond as expected to camera exposure adjustments, though there is a significant discrepancy in the brightness of the environment background.
|Camera exposure 0.1 (refprobe ambience) | Tonemap exposure 0.1 (refprobe ambience) |
| - | - |
| ![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/8881ed69-c868-4505-80e6-c5361f0bf990) | ![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/8d419cb2-940d-49e6-8e04-62d150699a62) |

Using lightmapGI, we see similar discrepancies:
| | Overexposed | Underexposed |
| - | - | - |
| Tonemap exposure | ![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/09492d19-772b-42f1-b5b7-d3587780bb1a) | ![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/31c61ef1-67cd-4026-90a1-1027ce77fcbc) |
| Camera exposure | ![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/bf0f3b7e-1f31-4996-ad96-20227ebea870) | ![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/ad2976e0-3115-413d-89e4-262b466ee168) |


When adjusting camera exposure, we can also regularly see ambient contributions getting updated, contrasting with the smooth exposure adjustment of the tonemap exposure. This is shown here using dynamic ambient lighting:

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/78eb27f6-aa9a-4474-8f72-262321994b0c


### Steps to reproduce

Create a scene with ambient lighting from sky, adjust exposure.

### Minimal reproduction project (MRP)

[ExposureMRP.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/19261597/ExposureMRP.zip)

summary: Fix error panel auto-hiding

description.text:
Fixes a minor regression introduced in https://github.com/godotengine/godot/commit/02cc187

Before this commit, the error panel would auto-hide if the error count dropped to zero, but would keep its previous state if there were still errors. When this change was made, the call to `_set_show_errors_panel` was put in the wrong place (can also be seen relative to the behaviour in `set_warning_count` below), meaning the error panel is automatically hidden when the error count changes as long as it is above 0, and makes it impossible to close the error panel when the error count reduces to 0.

Fixes #104147

summary: Flickering squares from positional lights when running a game through Wine

description.text:
### Tested versions v4.4.1.rc1.official [daa4b058e] Also reproducible with 4.3, 4.2 and 4.1 ### System information Godot v4.4.1.rc1 - macOS Sequoia (15.1.0) - Multi-window, 1 monitor - Metal (Forward+) - integrated Apple M1 Max (Apple7) - Apple M1 Max (10 threads) ### Issue description When running a windows build on macos through wine there are some flickering glitches: https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/e3d9ca1a-be81-46b0-a85a-d7b2303ef0ae - More lights = more flickering squares - Flickering squares change position when the window is resized - This happens regardless of whether shadows are enabled or disabled on those lights - This doesn't happen if there are no realtime lights in the scene (i.e only ambient light or lightmaps) - This doesn't seem to happen with directional light, only positional I noticed exactly the same when trying to run other people's Godot games Note: This could be stretching the limits of what is feasible for godot to track or fix, considering there's native export to both Linux and MacOS, but developers often don't bother exporting their games to anything besides Windows, in which case wine remains the only option. Also note that this is the only issue I see, everything else works perfectly well
For more context:

Worth mentioning that most Unity games don't even need wine to run on apple silicon because they run very well in Parallels VM. For example Stanley Parable UD (a fairly recent unity game) runs well at 2k 120fps in Parallels. For a Godot game to run in Parallels it needs to be built for arm64 explicitly and then it only works with Compatibility rendering, not Forward+

### Steps to reproduce Build the MRP for windows and run it through wine. I tested with Crossover 24 which is just a wine GUI. I can only test on apple silicon now but I'm curious if this is reproducible on Linux ### Minimal reproduction project (MRP) [mrp_wine_lights.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/19260178/mrp_wine_lights.zip)

summary: Unsaved script gets reloaded when Godot recovers focus

description.text:
### Tested versions

- Reproducible in: 4.4.stable and 4.4.1.rc1 (both official builds).

### System information

Godot v4.4.1.rc1 - Pop!_OS 22.04 LTS on X11 - X11 display driver, Multi-window, 1 monitor - Vulkan (Forward+) - integrated AMD Radeon Vega 8 Graphics (RADV RAVEN) - AMD Ryzen 5 3500U with Radeon Vega Mobile Gfx (8 threads)

### Issue description

I converted a project from `4.2` (I think at some point it was converted to `4.3` as well) to `4.4.stable`, and now everytime I focus out of Godot and come back to it, it tries to detect changes and reloads everything, which in the case of unsaved scripts results in the script losing pending changes and funnily enough it expands everything in the script.

I nuked `.godot` folder to try and fix the issue but didn't work.

### Steps to reproduce

Edit `node.gd` script without saving, make Godot lose focus by alt-tabbing to another program, or opening your OS notifications, or however you see fit. Come back to Godot and watch it reload the script wiping pending changes.

[mrp.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/19289445/mrp.zip)

summary: In the same script, a gutter icon is missing when a method in an inner class overriding the base one from another inner class.

description.text:
### Tested versions

4.5dev, but would be reproducible from 4.0 to 4.4.1(rc1)

### System information

Windows 11

### Issue description

Source from https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/103859#issuecomment-2721031078
I'd've thought that this was a "feature", but @HolonProduction told me on Rocket Chat that this would be a bug that should be fixed. So I post it here.


### Steps to reproduce

Copy the example script and paste it in the script editor of Godot
```GDScript
extends RefCounted

class A:
    var _t=1

    func _test() -> void:
        _t=2

class B extends A:
    func _test() -> void: # No gutter at the left side. Believe it or not.
        pass
```
And check if there is an overriding icon in the gutter

Since this is simple enough to reproduce, the MRP is the code itself.

When using Volumetric Fog with a Spotlight Node, the Volumetric Fog typically meets the Spotlight Node without Shadows enabled. With shadows on, the fog doesn't meet the node like it does without shadows, as demonstrated by the screenshots below (Green is without Shadows, Red is with Shadows).

2. Set `volumetric_fog_temporal_reprojection_enabled` to False in WorldEnvironment
3. Set `rendering/environment/volumetric_fog/use_filter` to False in Project Settings
